claim: All AI agents can be decomposed into 7 node types: LLM, Tool, Control, Memory, Guardrail, Fallback, and User Input nodes representing a graph-based architecture
actionable: When designing an AI agent, explicitly identify which of the 7 node types you need (e.g., long-term memory for context, guardrails for output validation) rather than building monolithically
source_title: Give Me 28 Minutes and I'll Completely Change the Way You Build AI Agents

claim: Control nodes add deterministic routing to otherwise non-deterministic agent workflows, preventing the need for agents to reason about which path to take
actionable: Use control nodes (conditional logic) for routing decisions based on agent output instead of asking the agent to decide which path to take, improving reliability
source_title: Give Me 28 Minutes and I'll Completely Change the Way You Build AI Agents

claim: Output guardrails with auto-fixing LLMs can validate agent responses and retry with feedback when output doesn't meet specified criteria
actionable: Implement an output guardrail node that checks agent responses against requirements (e.g., includes all fields) and automatically retries with critique feedback
source_title: Give Me 28 Minutes and I'll Completely Change the Way You Build AI Agents

claim: Memory nodes should be implemented at both start (retrieval) and end (storage) of workflows to manage long-term context without polluting short-term conversation history
actionable: For agents needing long-term memory, add memory retrieval at workflow start and memory extraction at end, using vector databases like those in Mem Zero
source_title: Give Me 28 Minutes and I'll Completely Change the Way You Build AI Agents

claim: Human-in-the-loop nodes paired with control nodes enable approval workflows for high-risk actions like booking hotels or sending emails before agent execution
actionable: For risky agent actions, add a user input node that interrupts workflow, requests approval, and feeds approval status to a control node for deterministic routing
source_title: Give Me 28 Minutes and I'll Completely Change the Way You Build AI Agents

claim: Fallback nodes handle errors consistently across the entire agent graph, enabling graceful degradation with default responses or retry logic
actionable: Implement a centralized error handling node that catches all agent failures and executes consistent fallback behavior (e.g., alert user, retry, or return default)
source_title: Give Me 28 Minutes and I'll Completely Change the Way You Build AI Agents

claim: Multiple LLM nodes with different tool sets can be connected as sub-agents to handle tool overload when a single agent exceeds 10-15 tools
actionable: When designing multi-MCP-server agents, wrap each MCP server in its own specialized sub-agent rather than giving one agent 30+ tools, preventing LLM confusion
source_title: Give Me 28 Minutes and I'll Completely Change the Way You Build AI Agents
